call_list.title: Ellis Medicine<>AI Call Center: Insurance Verification & Proposal Q&A
call_list.attendees: Brian Cummins, Wesley Sgroi, Bellowsm, Glenn Kunigan, romanop, Paulina Kaminski
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around discussing the integration and functionality of a proposed solution for insurance verification and call center operations.

Topics Discussed:
Call Center Operations
Wesley Sgroi inquired about the current status of the call center, noting it was busy but typical for a Fri
call_list.pain_point:
Buyer’s current insurance verification process is multi-step and time-consuming, requiring schedulers and financial counselors to manually interpret data.
Calls in the call center take too long, leading to complaints despite not dropping calls.
Buyer needs to automate insurance verification to reduce human touches and speed up scheduling.
Current system requires manual initiation of insurance verification and manual reading of complex data.
Difficulty in interpreting primary vs specialty copays and different copay levels.
Challenges with payer websites and data availability, especially for key local payers (Fidelis, CDPHP, MVP).
Existing AI solutions only get about 80-85% of the way, lacking full automation.
Need to avoid additional platforms or landing pages that add complexity and cost.
Buyer’s CFO is concerned about contract length (three-year lock-in) and pricing.
Buyer must ensure any solution is budget neutral and shows clear ROI.
Need for better organization and summarization of insurance data to avoid human parsing.
Need for integration with Cerner as source of truth without duplicating data.
Need for AI to infer context (e.g., appointment type) and suggest financial responsibility automatically.
Need for faster scheduling and insurance verification in one step rather than two.
Need to handle interpreter needs and patient preferences efficiently.
Concern about vendor solutions requiring additional platforms or add-ons increasing cost.
Need to balance functionality with cost and practicality for a small community hospital.
Need to avoid giving out personal cell phone numbers to clients to reduce complexity.
Need to handle multiple vendors and proposals for comparison.
Need to ensure AI agents and copilot can effectively assist human agents and patients.
Need to avoid solutions that require separate landing pages or platforms not integrated with telephony.
Need to ensure solution can handle all payer data including Medicaid HMOs and Medicare details.
Need to reduce call length and improve patient experience with faster verification.

call_list.title: Enloe <> Innovaccer
call_list.opportunity: Enloe - Prior Auth, Enloe Medical Center_Comet
call_list.attendees: osteeto, Dan Syse, Kevin Woodward, Lisa Stark, Glenn Kunigan, Caitlin Spargo
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around discussing AI-driven solutions for improving scheduling, referral management, and authorization processes within healthcare settings.

Topics Discussed:
Meeting Dynamics
Lisa Stark and Dan Syse discussed the challenges of back-to-back meetings and the preference for longe
call_list.pain_point:
Seller asks about challenges in access centers: "It's probably the same across the board long. Hold times. Referral leakage. Staffing shortages. Rising administrative costs."
Buyer describes scheduling utilization challenges: "Right now our current Schedule utilization within epic is a challenge for us. In some of our ambulatory areas, the Clinic's don't necessarily use their schedules the same way."
Buyer notes infrastructure work needed to prepare for technology use: "There's a lot of infrastructure work that we would have to do in order to prepare us to be able to utilize this technology."
Buyer mentions complexity in ambulatory scheduling preventing Epic auto-scheduling use: "Epic has capability of letting patients Auto schedule with an epic, right? We haven't done it in a lot of areas because it's become really complex."
Seller highlights multiple challenges in access centers: "Hold times, referral leakage, staffing shortages, rising administrative costs."
Buyer notes manual processing of imaging referrals: "The piece was around Of that manually processing of Imaging referrals."
Buyer mentions multiple entry points and complexity in call centers: "There's a lot of different entry points. So what Caitlyn will do? She'll kind of walk through and let me know of one area is more important to start."
Buyer notes need for synchronization of schedules to avoid conflicts: "I have to have immediate ability to lock in that patient because there's either another AI scheduling agent or there's somebody who just met with the physician and they're now checking out. And if we don't immediately lock that down, then that somebody else can take that appointment. And then there's a conflict that is only realized after the fact."

call_list.title: Internal-UMMS Potential Pilot for Referral Copilot
call_list.attendees: Brian Cummins, Keg Avakian
call_list.summary:
The meeting between Keg Avakian and Brian Cummins focused on discussing the challenges and opportunities related to referral management and AI solutions. The conversation revolved around the financial viability and strategic approach for implementing these solutions.

Topics Discussed:
Referral Mana
call_list.pain_point:
Referral volume at the buyer's organization is very small, limiting revenue potential ("The challenge with umms is that their referral volume is Tiny.")
Margins on AI solutions are low or potentially negative due to high costs ("our margins at that level are not great.", "I can't even guarantee you that we'd be margin positive.")
Pilot implementations require significant effort and resources despite small deal size ("every time I sign up to do one of these that's capacity that I'm not going to have...")
Pricing per referral is high compared to what the customer is willing to pay ("we'd be going from like something like four or five dollars a referral to like $34 a referral, which I'm just guessing is probably not going to hunt.")
AI solutions require ongoing hands-on management and customization for each customer ("every single time you start with the new customer, you got to learn all the quirks of their business...")
Competitive pressure in referral management is low, but pricing is commoditized in other areas ("there's so many other competitors that they're all offering bargain-basement pricing to try to get market share.")
Difficulty in making small pilots financially viable ("trying to do yet an additional like favor of making it like a free pilot just feels really tough.")
Customers are not interested in partial solutions, preferring more complete offerings ("The customer wasn't interested in just buying the agents. So we're selling them a more complete version of Access Center.")

call_list.title: Phillip Craig Hethcox - Surgery Partners <> Innovaccer
call_list.attendees: I_gunpreet Kour, pch001, Alex Garcia
call_list.summary:
The meeting centered around discussing potential solutions for reducing the high cancellation rates at surgery centers, particularly focusing on the integration of AI technology to improve patient engagement and retention.

Topics Discussed:
Introduction and Background
Alex Garcia introduced the mee
call_list.pain_point:
Seller asks about cancellation issues and patient no-shows.
Buyer states: "Cancellation rate is 18%, with many patients disappearing after scheduling surgery."
Buyer states: "Nine people cancel out of 40 scheduled surgeries, causing overstaffing and operational inefficiencies."
Buyer states: "Patients often don't respond to texts or emails, sometimes say they will come but then no-show."
Buyer states: "Cancellation problem has increased in 2023-2025, partly due to economic pressures and high deductibles."
Buyer states: "Patients have high out-of-pocket costs, e.g., deductibles up to $7000, which affects surgery decisions."
Buyer states: "Patients don't know the surgery center well, never met the staff, leading to lack of engagement and cancellations."
Buyer states: "Revenue cycle management and patient follow-up processes are challenging and contribute to the problem."

Potential difficulties in managing referral leakage and appointment no-shows.

Need for better visibility and coordination across franchise clinics and provider networks.
